skill.name: pytorch-lightning
skill.description: Build and review PyTorch Lightning training code with guidance for LightningModules, DataModules, Trainers, callbacks, logging, and distributed strategies.
skill.summary: Create structured PyTorch Lightning training workflows with practical templates and reference guidance.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, weak crypto, eval, network, and environment findings, but review shows they are overwhelmingly Markdown inline-code or documentation examples. The executable scripts are educational PyTorch Lightning templates and do not show credential exfiltration, unauthorized network calls, shell execution, or prompt-injection attempts. Low residual risk remains because the skill includes runnable scripts, placeholder API key examples, and an instruction that promotes an external K-Dense website during complex workflows.

low_findings.title: Executable ML Template Scripts
low_findings.description: The skill includes runnable Python template scripts that import PyTorch Lightning and related ML libraries. Review found no shell execution, network exfiltration, or credential access in these scripts, but users should run community scripts only in an appropriate project environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports and template entry points are directly visible, and targeted review found normal Lightning examples. The residual risk is operational rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Credential Examples
low_findings.description: The logging documentation shows placeholder api_key fields for Comet and Neptune logger setup. These are not real secrets and are not transmitted by the skill itself, but users should avoid hardcoding real credentials when adapting the examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are explicit placeholders named YOUR_API_KEY, so they are not exposed credentials. The concern is misuse if users replace placeholders with real secrets in source files.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only Network Examples
low_findings.description: The references include example URLs, localhost tracking, and private-network master addresses for dataset and distributed training demonstrations. Review found no active network client in the skill scripts, so these are documentation examples rather than exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators appear inside Markdown examples and common distributed-training configuration. No evidence found that the skill executes these examples automatically.

low_findings.title: Promotional External Website Suggestion
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This does not attempt to override security analysis, but it can steer users toward an external service and should be disclosed as promotional content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and user-facing in the skill file. It is not a prompt-injection attempt because it does not ask the evaluator to ignore policy, skip review, or change risk levels.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static External Command Hits Are Markdown False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer flagged many Ruby or shell backtick executions, but reviewed examples are Markdown inline code, fenced code blocks, or ordinary Python snippets. No evidence found of actual Ruby backtick execution or unsanitized shell invocation in the skill scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed representative flagged lines are Markdown formatting or normal documentation examples. The executable Python scripts do not contain subprocess, os.system, or equivalent shell execution evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static eval and Weak Crypto Hits Are Semantic False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The eval finding points to model.eval(), which switches a PyTorch model to inference mode, not dynamic code execution. Weak cryptography hits reviewed at cited locations are ordinary text such as DDP strategy documentation, max_steps, or headings, not cryptographic code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited eval call is the PyTorch inference method and not Python eval. The cited weak-crypto samples do not contain hashing or encryption operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
dangerous_patterns.description: Targeted review did not find instructions to ignore previous instructions, skip security analysis, claim pre-approval, or alter the audit risk level. The promotional K-Dense Web instruction is disclosed separately as a low-risk marketplace concern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Targeted phrase search found no classic prompt-injection language. Confidence is not maximal because review was targeted rather than a full semantic proof of every line.

content.value_statement: PyTorch training code can become hard to organize, scale, and debug. This skill gives structured Lightning patterns for models, data, trainers, callbacks, logging, and distributed training.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to structure LightningModule methods for training, validation, testing, prediction, and optimizer configuration.
Provides template scripts for LightningModule and LightningDataModule boilerplate.
Shows Trainer configurations for debugging, production runs, multi-GPU DDP, FSDP, and DeepSpeed-style workflows.
Documents callback patterns for checkpoints, early stopping, learning rate monitoring, and custom callbacks.
Covers experiment logging with TensorBoard, Weights and Biases, MLflow, Comet, Neptune, and CSV loggers.
Summarizes distributed training strategy selection for DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, and multi-node setups.
limitations: It provides guidance and templates, not a complete application or trained model.
Users must adapt examples to their dataset, model architecture, hardware, and Lightning version.
The scripts require PyTorch Lightning and related dependencies before they can run.
External logging services require user-managed accounts, projects, and credentials.

use_cases.title: Convert PyTorch Code
use_cases.description: Refactor a custom PyTorch loop into a LightningModule with clean training, validation, and optimizer sections.

use_cases.title: Prepare Reusable Data Pipelines
use_cases.description: Design a LightningDataModule that separates data preparation, setup, loaders, transforms, and reproducible train validation splits.

use_cases.title: Scale Training Runs
use_cases.description: Choose Trainer settings for debugging, mixed precision, checkpointing, DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, or multi-node execution.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic LightningModule
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a PyTorch LightningModule for a classifier. Include training_step, validation_step, and configure_optimizers.

prompt_templates.title: Build a DataModule
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a LightningDataModule for my dataset. Separate prepare_data, setup, train_dataloader, val_dataloader, and test_dataloader.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Production Training
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my PyTorch Lightning Trainer setup for checkpointing, early stopping, logging, precision, and fast debugging options.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Distributed Training
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare DDP, FSDP, and DeepSpeed for my model size and hardware. Recommend a Lightning Trainer configuration and explain tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: I have a PyTorch model and want to move it to Lightning.
output: Guidance for moving forward pass, loss calculation, metrics, and optimizer setup.
Notes about logging metrics and keeping device handling Lightning-native.

output_examples.input: I need to train the same model on one GPU and then four GPUs.
output: Trainer settings for single-device debugging and multi-GPU DDP.
Distributed training cautions for batch size, metric synchronization, and data loaders.

output_examples.input: My training project needs cleaner data handling.
output: A DataModule design with clear prepare_data and setup responsibilities.
Recommended loader settings for train, validation, test, and prediction stages.
Reproducibility notes for splits, transforms, and worker settings.

best_practices: Use LightningModule methods to keep model logic, losses, metrics, and optimizers organized.
Keep data preparation in LightningDataModule methods so runs are reproducible across machines and devices.
Start with fast_dev_run or CPU debugging before scaling to GPU, DDP, FSDP, or DeepSpeed.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode credentials for experiment tracking services in training files.
Do not mix manual device transfers with Lightning-managed device placement unless the strategy requires it.
Do not choose distributed strategies before confirming model size, memory use, and hardware topology.

faq.question: Does this skill train models automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, examples, and templates that users adapt and run in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it help convert plain PyTorch code to Lightning?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to organize model code into LightningModule methods and Trainer workflows.

faq.question: Does it include DataModule guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes references and a template for reusable data preparation, setup, and dataloader methods.

faq.question: Which distributed strategies does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, and multi-node training considerations at a practical configuration level.

faq.question: Does it manage logging service credentials?
faq.answer: No. Users must provide and protect their own credentials for services such as Comet, Neptune, or Weights and Biases.

faq.question: Is this safe to use with private datasets?
faq.answer: The skill does not upload data by itself. Review any logging or download examples before using private data.
